challenge_questions: Does the horizon capture intraday peaks and multi-day persistence?
Are resource monetization times aligned with obligation timing?
Is the horizon conservative under delayed receipts and rollover failure?
missing_keyword_groups: same-day | available when needed | timely availability
results.name: Liquidity horizon
observations: Intraday and multi-day points are ordered.
The horizon extends beyond time zero.
results.remediation: Document time buckets, peak-need selection, multi-day persistence, and resource monetization timing.
results.risk_statement: An understated or timing-inconsistent horizon can omit the peak liquidity need.

challenge_questions: Are contractual, contingent, and stressed cash flows separately defined?
Are signs, units, timing, and aggregation conventions unambiguous?
Does the integrated requirement prevent duplicate recognition?
results.name: Cash-flow definitions
observations: Integrated components use unique identifiers.
Cash-flow magnitudes are nonnegative.
results.remediation: Create a controlled cash-flow dictionary with sign, timing, source, unit, and anti-double-counting rules.
results.risk_statement: Ambiguous definitions or duplicated components can materially misstate stressed requirements.

challenge_questions: Are Cover 1 and Cover 2 selected from scenario-specific stressed needs?
Can affiliated or correlated members be represented without duplication?
Are default sets reproducible and configurable?
results.name: Default-set construction
observations: Cover 1 selects the largest unique member.
Cover 2 selects the largest two unique members.
results.remediation: Base selection on scenario-specific member requirements, enforce uniqueness, and retain ranked member evidence.
results.risk_statement: Incorrect default-set logic can select the wrong liquidity-driving member combination.

challenge_questions: Are yield shocks converted using consistent decimal and basis-point units?
Are duration and convexity signs correct for long and short positions?
Are curve and key-rate shocks mapped to maturity buckets without gaps?
results.name: Yield-to-price methodology
observations: A positive yield shock reduces price.
A negative yield shock increases price.
Convexity is represented in nonlinear shocks.
results.remediation: Retain benchmark calculations, sign tests, key-rate mappings, and independent price-change reconciliation.
results.risk_statement: Unit, sign, or mapping errors can reverse or materially distort Treasury liquidation losses.

challenge_questions: Are failure rates severity-dependent, bounded, and empirically defensible?
Are lender withdrawal and refinancing-horizon assumptions independent where appropriate?
Does the model avoid treating failed rollover and haircut demand as the same cash flow?
results.name: Repo rollover assumptions
observations: Rollover-failure rates increase with severity.
Rollover-failure rates remain bounded.
results.remediation: Calibrate bounded monotonic rates, document lender concentration, and separate principal replacement from incremental cost.
results.risk_statement: Unsubstantiated rollover assumptions can understate replacement funding and concentration risk.

challenge_questions: Are baseline and stressed haircuts conservative and maturity-sensitive?
Are concentration effects applied once and in the correct sequence?
Can additional collateral exceed available unencumbered inventory?
results.name: Haircut assumptions
observations: Haircuts increase across maturity or stress buckets.
Haircuts remain economically bounded.
results.remediation: Document haircut hierarchy, caps, concentration sequence, and inventory constraints with boundary tests.
results.risk_statement: Haircut misspecification can understate collateral calls or double count concentration effects.

challenge_questions: Are fails-to-receive and fails-to-deliver treated asymmetrically where required?
Are recoveries credited only when timely and reliable?
Are persistent fails carried forward without duplicating original settlement obligations?
results.name: Settlement-fail treatment
observations: Replacement liquidity is net of reliable recoveries.
Persistent multi-day fails do not reduce the requirement.
results.remediation: Introduce obligation identifiers, persistence roll-forward logic, and explicit recovery eligibility.
results.risk_statement: Incorrect fail treatment can omit replacement liquidity or count the same obligation multiple times.

challenge_questions: Can every counted resource be accessed in the relevant currency and time bucket?
Are encumbrance, haircuts, provider reliability, and operational capacity reflected?
Are contingent or uncommitted resources excluded from available qualified resources?
results.name: Resource eligibility
observations: Eligibility, encumbrance, haircut, and availability are applied.
results.remediation: Maintain a resource eligibility register with legal, operational, currency, timing, haircut, and encumbrance attributes.
results.risk_statement: Counting unavailable or ineligible resources can create a false LCR surplus.

challenge_questions: Is netting supported by enforceable rules for the modeled default state?
Are only compatible currency, date, product, and settlement-system obligations netted?
Does the model revert to gross obligations when criteria fail?
missing_keyword_groups: bilateral netting | multilateral netting | netting set
results.name: Netting assumptions
observations: Permitted netting reduces matched obligations.
Gross fallback applies when enforceability is absent.
results.remediation: Define legal netting sets, operational compatibility criteria, and an automatic gross fallback.
results.risk_statement: Overly broad netting can materially suppress peak settlement liquidity.

challenge_questions: Are scenarios monotonic where expected and non-redundant?
Is extreme-but-plausible severity supported by historical or hypothetical rationale?
Do combined scenarios preserve coherent dependencies rather than simply summing maxima?
results.name: Scenario severity
observations: Scenario severity is monotonic.
Extreme-but-plausible severity exceeds severe stress.
results.remediation: Document severity calibration, dependence structure, historical anchors, and reverse-stress thresholds.
results.risk_statement: Weak or incoherent scenarios can fail to expose the model's true liquidity vulnerability.

challenge_questions: Do synthetic members exactly reconcile to controlled Federal Reserve aggregates?
Are concentration and tail behavior intentional, reproducible, and documented?
Is any actual FICC participant identity or participant-level inference excluded?
missing_keyword_groups: financing and fails reconciliation | fails reconciliation
results.name: Synthetic-member calibration
observations: Synthetic members reconcile exactly to the source total.
Synthetic identifiers are explicit.
Deterministic calibration reproduces fixed proportions.
results.remediation: Retain reconciliation tables, deterministic seeds, distribution diagnostics, and explicit synthetic-data controls.
results.risk_statement: Poor calibration can invalidate member concentration, default-set, and Cover 1/Cover 2 conclusions.
